title: How to Grow Courgettes/Zucchini: 4 Months of Cropping from an April Sowing Under Cover
text:
Courgettes can crop for four months or more, from one April sowing under cover. 

In this video I show how I start them in rich compost and explain why there’s no rush to sow too early, as they are susceptible to frost. I talk about using fleece for protection against spring’s cold winds — sometimes double layers if needed — in the UK, this is the 30gsm fleece I use https://charlesdowding.co.uk/products/fleece?variant=54252843204931

I share tips on planting distance and watering to keep growth steady. And my favourite variety is Defender F1 https://premierseedsdirect.com/product/courgette-defender-f1-charles/

I explain why I grow F1 varieties, when to remove the very first small fruits to encourage stronger cropping later, and how to harvest so that plants continue producing. We also look at the natural silver-grey colouring on leaves, which is often mistaken for powdery mildew, and how to keep plants productive through many months.

With steady warmth, courgettes crop heavily through summer and into early autumn — often giving one family more than enough from just one or two plants.

title: Small Garden Plantings and Soil Maintenance in Early Spring | No Dig
text:
Early spring in a small garden is a time of promise, but also patience. In this no dig garden I share what is growing now, what I am planting in early spring, and just as importantly, what I am not planting yet.

It's eight months since we were here last, because I missed the autumn through having lymphoma cancer. Adam did a great job of caring for the gardens last autumn.

You can see how small the new plants are at the moment. It's still early for many plantings, and if you are in a cooler climate, there is no rush. 

My new book, Grow Together, out on 9th April but available for pre-order: https://charlesdowding.co.uk/products/grow-together-50-planting-partnerships-to-boost-your-harvests

title: No Dig Garden Tour | What We've Planted and What’s Next to Sow
text:
What can you plant now, and what is still too early to sow or plant out? In this tour just after the spring equinox, I show you what is growing well here at Homeacres and share a few timely spring planting tips.

Spring can feel full of promise, but the weather is still unsettled and frost is still possible here in our zone 8 climate. You’ll see lettuce, wild rocket, radish, broad beans, carrots, spring cabbage, kohlrabi, peas, leeks and salads, with thoughts on watering, spacing, direct sowing and module planting.

I also explain why patience is necessary for frost-tender crops, and how fleece and covers can protect young plants from wind, cold and pests.

title: How to Grow Sweetcorn: Best Varieties, Sowing Times & Harvest Tips
text:
Sweetcorn has to be one of the most worthwhile things to grow at home. In this video I show you different types of sweetcorn, when to sow, how to protect plants from cold, and how to harvest at the right moment for the best taste.

Sweetcorn needs decent summer warmth to grow, say afternoons over 21°C, 70°F. In the UK this is not guaranteed, even here in the south, but it happens that this video was made in 2025, an unusually warm summer here, so you're seeing some great results.

It may not always be so easy! Likewise, a late frost spells disaster, so I explain how to protect plants using fleece for the first two to four weeks. This is a UK link for the 30gsm fleece I use https://gardening-naturally.com/collections/garden-fleece/products/30g-fleece

title: 3 Ways to Grow Vegetables for Early Spring Harvests
text:
Want earlier harvests in spring? In this short video, I share 3 simple ways to grow for early harvests, including autumn sowing, early spring planting under fleece, and when to use hoops for extra frost protection. 

These are practical methods I use here at Homeacres.
1 is to sow suitable vegetables in autumn, such as the broad / fave beans here.
2 is to sow late February, then plant a month later and cover with fleece on top.
3 is similar to 2 except for supporting the plants on hoops.

title: Quick Way to Increase Stem Strength When Transplanting
text:
A quick way to transplant long-stem seedlings more deeply in a no dig bed, so stems are sheltered and plants establish strongly.

In the soft surface of a no dig bed, it's quick and easy to make a slightly deeper hole for transplanting vegetables and flowers with long stems. There are supported by the surrounding soil and compost - but notice that I don't fill the hole completely, it fills with time and rainfall. 

You can find the dibbers like this here https://charlesdowding.co.uk/products/long-handled-ash-dibber?variant=49015163191619

title: How to Grow Leeks for Harvests in All Four Seasons
text:
As summer cedes to autumn, leek growth gets underway from spring sowings that we transplanted in early summer. Then suddenly their lovely distinctive leaves are tall, and rise to prominence in the garden as summer crops fade. 

For eating, they offer sweet blanched stems, together with perhaps the only fresh green leaves in frosty winters. Leeks’ season of maturity extends over nine months, and best results come from sowing the right variety to harvest in each season within this period.

Here I grow Jolant for early harvests from September, https://premierseedsdirect.com/product/leek-jolant-charles/
and Bandit https://premierseedsdirect.com/product/leek-bandit-charles/
for late ones until early May.

The module tray I use is https://charlesdowding.co.uk/products/module-tray-cd15?variant=49015166435651
The copper trowel https://charlesdowding.co.uk/products/copper-tools?variant=53869775978819
The fleece cover https://charlesdowding.co.uk/products/biofleece-plastic-free-fleece?variant=49013317435715
Leek Growing Guide https://charlesdowding.co.uk/products/leeks-growing-guide?variant=49139109953859

Allium leaf miner is a 6mm (1/4") white maggot of the even smaller leaf mining fly, which lays eggs on all alliums in both early spring and autumn. Damage shows as lines of white spots on infected leaves, followed by them rotting, especially onion leaves in June and leeks in late autumn. By then you may notice some dark orange pupae of 4mm length. Apart from squashing any maggots and pupae you find, there are few certain remedies. The best is to cover with fleece or very fine mesh, from planting time and for about ten weeks.

title: What to Sow in March | Hardy Vegetable Seeds to Start Now
text:
March is a wonderful month for sowing vegetables, especially the cold-loving ones that tolerate frost. In this short video I share a selection of seeds you can start now, including lettuce, onions, peas, beetroot, coriander, parsley and more. Timing matters though — some crops such as cucumbers, sweetcorn and summer beans are better left until April, so it's helpful to know what to prioritise now.

See my 2026 Calendar for sowing dates throughout 2026.

title: Broad Beans in Winter: The Difference Bird Netting Makes | No Dig
text:
You can sow broad beans in early November, in this zone 8 climate which has mild winters with frosts no lower than -8°C this year, for example. Survival has been excellent and more than that, a simple cover of bird netting has reduced wind impact on these beans. 
The net looks insignificant but it clearly makes a really worthwhile difference.

title: Seed to Harvest Celeriac through 7 Months (No Dig Guide)
text:
Celeriac is not an easy vegetable to grow. Quite often people tell me how theirs has stayed small, and there are usually clear reasons for that. It’s a long-season crop, and success comes down to timing, spacing and watering through the summer.

Timings need to be spot-on, from sowing in mid March to planting early May and using a cover over the plants for the first six weeks. Then harvest in autumn before it's too wet.

In this video I show the full seven-month journey, from sowing tiny light-germinating seeds in mid-March and pricking out at the two-leaf stage, through to transplanting at generous spacing and harvesting in October and November. Because the seed is so small, early care makes all the difference.

Celeriac does not like to be cramped, so I plant at around 35cm spacing to allow roots to swell properly. I water well at planting, then hold back in early summer, increasing moisture later when the bulbs are swelling. Too much water early on can lead to softer growth and more risk of septoria in autumn.

Growing no dig, with compost mulch on the surface, helps retain moisture and feed soil life while keeping disturbance minimal. I also show companion planting with dwarf French marigolds (Tagetes patula), second cropping after radish, and a simple harvesting method that leaves most roots in the soil to feed microbes.

By autumn, roots can reach 800g to 1kg (around 2lb). There’s no rush to harvest early — they continue swelling into late October and November. Harvest before winter really sets in to avoid leaf disease spreading into the bulb, then store in boxes in a cool shed for delicious winter meals.

In Homeacres No Dig Garden, Somerset, UK

title: Simple and good value way to improve water quality
text:
Easy to apply, the two halves of Abimax magnets hold each other in place around a pipe or hose. This creates a magnetic field between the two halves, and when water flows through that, the scale in hard water, which is calcite, changes to aragonite, which does not stick. 

So nothing is removed, but the difficult results of hard water are not there anymore, and that keeps water flowing easily through equipment such as pipes and kettles. Water becomes 'structured', more usable by plants, and more hydrating for us as well.

Find these devices with my affiliate link at https://www.abimax.com/pages/garden?ref=CharlesDowding, and apply NODIG at checkout for 10% off.

title: Seedling Kindergarten in Early Spring: Germination Heat, Light & Strong Growth
text:
Tiny seedlings need special care!
I share all the tricks I have learnt.
An extra one is the additional heat required for germination, and how that does not need light, except for celery and celeriac. So I germinate seeds in my house, where it's warmer at night than the greenhouse. You can do this even with trays stacked on top of one another, because light is not vital at that stage. 

Then as soon as you see seedling leaves, bring trays to full light, or as full as possible to prevent them growing thin, leggy stems. That's the point at which this video really kicks in, and I hope that my understandings help you to succeed your seedlings.

title: Sow Your Seeds Now! What to Start Under Cover in Late February | No Dig
text:
Tips for successful propagation.

This late February is such a good time for under cover sowing of vegetables that tolerate cold, and can even survive light frost.

They include lettuce, onions, spring onions, peas for pods, and peas for shoots, broad or lava beans, cabbage of early varieties, spinach, fennel, kohlrabi, celery, and more. See my other videos for further information.

Also my website Sowing Timeline https://charlesdowding.co.uk/blogs/no-dig-resources/sowing-timeline

Late winter sowing is one of the most productive moments in the gardening year. With a little protection under cover, these hardy vegetables establish strongly, giving you earlier harvests and healthy, resilient plants. It’s a simple way to get ahead in the no dig garden and make the most of increasing light levels.

title: February Garden Tour: Unusual Late Winter Growth | No Dig Update
text:
Transition to spring is underway, with a lovely combination of daylight increasing rapidly and temperatures rising above normal. February this year has daytime average temperatures 10.6°C and at night time 5.4°C, with only one frost. Rainfall is 100 mm or 9 inches. The long term average figures are 8.5°C and 1.4°C by night, with 68mm rain and 10 frosts.

Thanks to no dig there are few weeds, and we do a little weeding if any appear. That's important so that beds are ready for new seeds and plants, with slug numbers kept low. All beds are mulched with 2–3cm compost, paths with 2 cm woodchip of less than 12 mm diameter.

title: Grow Tender Celery, Watering, Blanching & No Dig
text:
Celery grows best where surface moisture is abundant, to grow crisp stems with less stringy fibres. Frequent watering is better than occasional soaks, and it's one vegetable that I never skimp on when watering, because the more you give it the lusher and crisper are the plants.

Raise plants indoors: sow seed on the surface of compost in a seed tray and cover with glass to retain moisture. Water with a fine rose about once a week. Seedlings need two weeks to germinate, and you can prick them into 3cm (1") modules, after two to three weeks.

Lay a mesh cover over new plantings, to keep aphids away and give faster growth.

Celery tastes better for some blanching to reduce its bitterness. Self-blanching works by close planting to exclude light around stems, with a dense canopy of leaves above them. Blanching with ridges of soil is messy, time consuming, not no dig, and results in extra slug holes.

In this video I share practical tips on growing tender celery in a no dig garden — from sowing seed and steady watering, to mesh protection and simple self-blanching methods for crisp, flavourful stems.

title: When and How to Sow and Grow Kohlrabi
text:
Often referred to as a niche crop, my preference would be to put it centre stage. In appearance it's almost unreal, but choose the right variety for the season and you have a staple vegetable of two seasons, early summer and late autumn, even through winter.

Although it looks like a root, kohlrabi is a swelling of the plant stem just above ground level but with still some stem underneath, so the appearance is similar to flying saucers in the garden. They have a sweet taste and delicate texture, as long as we pick them before any elongation of the bulb, which is a move towards flowering and eventually results in woodiness. 

It’s a classic half-season crop: the time to maturity from sowing date is 10 weeks in June and 14 in autumn, when there's more time to admire them as they swell, without  worrying about bolting because flowering of summer sown kohlrabi does not happen until the spring. The bulbs are frost hardy to maybe -6°C, so you can leave them in the ground until it turns proper cold.

title: Earthworm Castings: A Clear Sign of Healthy Soil Structure
text:
Big excitement here. What, what could possibly be going on here? Earthworms, fertilising the soil. Limbricus terrestris. This is the beauty of leaving them undisturbed, they do all this work for us all the time.

Earthworm activity is what we want plenty of in our soils and this is one sign of it, the surface casts with a hole underneath. They are different to Brandling worms which you find in wormeries

title: Grow Early Fennel 🌱 From Sowing to Harvest | No Dig Gardening
text:
Florence fennel, also known as bulb fennel, is grown for its tender bulbs and is quite different from herb fennels. In this video I show how fennel is sown, grown and harvested in a no dig garden.

Florence or bulb fennel, is an umbellifer of the same family as carrots, parsnips, celeriac and chervil. Eaten raw, the sweet aniseed flavour is refreshing in summer and lends character to salads. Gentle cooking brings out a sweet and creamy quality, delicious in soups, ratatouille or on its own with garlic and parsley. The feathery leaves are edible, with a dill-like flavour.

This bulbing vegetable is completely different to the types of long stemmed fennel, grown as a herb.They are cultivars of this same plant and are often called ‘fennel’, which results in confusion. They are perennial and can grow large, also they drop seeds at the end of summer which can become a weed problem. Best solution is to harvest the seeds in September before they fall, for cooking.

Bulbs will start to swell on the surface after about six weeks, and if all goes well they fatten to over 450g (1lb). Any bolting tendency would initially show as elongation of small bulbs, before or at which point they are best cut and eaten. Trim off all stalks to enjoy the unusual and rich flavours.

I sow fennel into module trays, using about 60% homemade compost, with the rest being proprietary compost, and the fennel seed is quite large and easy to handle.
You can succeed fennel with one bulb per station about nine inches, or 22 centimetres, apart, and if you have more plants together they will grow, but you’ll get smaller bulbs.

title: Spring Onions from Seed to Harvest
text:
Spring onions grow quickly and are ready to harvest in a short time. In this video, I show how I grow spring onions from seed to harvest.

Salad onions are varieties that have been bred to make a white stem with firm leaves, harvested before bulbing. The common name of ‘spring onion’ reveals how they are at their best in spring, before leaf diseases appear, and because they were often the only onion available in spring, between old and new crops.

Imagine delicious fresh green onion leaves and stems as early as spring equinox – a fine way to enliven early salads, soups and many other dishes. They are from sowing a salad variety, such as White Lisbon Winter Hardy, in late August. Spring-sown salad onions, sown February to April, will not be ready until May or June, depending how large you want them to grow. Left to grow into bulbs, they are still tasty raw or cooked.

title: How to Grow Spinach | Best Sowing Times, Varieties & No Dig Growing
text:
Spinach is one of the most rewarding leafy greens to grow, especially in late winter, spring and autumn. In this video, I share the best sowing times, how to raise strong plants, and which spinach varieties crop longest using simple no dig methods. Medania grows well here, see what you think.

You’ll learn when to sow spinach in spring and late summer, how to avoid bolting (flowering), why multi-sowing in modules works so well, and how spinach performs through cold, frost and winter damp.

True spinach (not leaf beet) is so delicious, in winter and spring above all. First for salad, and as a cooked green simply allow leaves to grow larger, with perhaps a few slug holes appearing in the process, but conveniently disappearing again in cooking!

Keep plants easy to pick by removing yellowing leaves to compost, also that affords less habitat for slugs under ground-level foliage. Slugs make it difficult to grow spinach from direct sowing. Therefore I mostly raise plants indoors, three seeds per 3 cm/1 in module. One tray of 30 or even 60 module plants will yield substantial pickings.

Best sowing dates are late February to March, then from late July for overwintered spinach, and until the third week in August. For seeds to sow, leave plants unpicked through spring and that helps seed to develop and be more viable. Sometime from late July you should be able to start rubbing out some dry seed and when you can do quite a bit of that from one plant, cut just below ground level to have stems for hanging initially under cover.

title: New book: Grow Together – Companion Planting and Intercropping for Productive Gardens
text:
Pre-order link https://geni.us/GrowTogether
I am so excited to finally hold my new book Grow Together in my hands 🌱 It arrived through the letterbox this morning. 

This book is all about growing more food in the same space, using companion planting, inter-sowing and overlapping crops. I’ve been practising this for many years, and it’s about getting the timings right – making the most of the end of one crop while the next one is just beginning.

A simple example is carrots followed by Brussels sprouts. Sow carrots in late March, then in mid-June thin a few and plant in your Brussels. Keep the mesh on, harvest carrots through July, and you’ll have strong Brussels ready for winter.

Grow Together is published by Dorling Kindersley.

title: Growing Cauliflower | Sowing, Spacing & Harvest Timing | No Dig Gardening
text:
Cauliflowers are difficult to grow well, and highly rewarding when it comes off. You need to keep an eye out as harvest approaches, not to let them grow too mature.

There is a choice of colours, more than what I show here, including green and yellow. Cauliflower is a vegetable that you can grow right through the year. It can help to match the variety you’re sowing to the time of year, some do better in spring and some do better in autumn, although I’ve found over the years that there’s not a huge difference actually.

First sowing is mid February in temperate climates, and I recommend a second sowing in early May, followed by a third and final one around solstice time. That's for autumn harvest. You can also experiment with sowing early September to set out small plants for cropping in Spring. I'm trying that this year but there is no guarantee of success!

In this video, cauliflower growing is followed from sowing through to harvest, using no dig methods. With the one I’m sowing, called White Excel, I’ve had fantastic crops in the autumn from sowing in summer. I’m sowing into module trays, sometimes more than one seed per cell, and then thinning to the strongest.

Cold nights are what slows germination or even stops it, so the trays are brought into the house for germination and then moved back to the greenhouse once they have emerged.
I show cauliflower seedlings four weeks on, with strong germination, helped by being a hybrid.
Where there are doubles, they can be separated, but cauliflowers do better grown on their own.
Seedlings that are noticeably weaker are best discarded.

The cauliflowers are planted into a no dig bed with compost already on the surface and without adding fertiliser. Spacing is about avoiding cramming plants in too close, or spacing them so wide that space is wasted.

As harvest approaches, cauliflowers need watching carefully because it’s a one-off harvest.
Just because a cauliflower looks small doesn’t mean it isn’t ready. If left too long, it will open out into looser florets and the stems become tougher. You can peel back the leaves to see what’s happening inside. The cauliflower is cut, and the outer leaves are edible as well.

title: How to Grow Asparagus | From Seed or Crowns No-Dig Advice
text:
Growing asparagus is deeply rewarding, whether you choose to sow seed or plant crowns.
From seed works very well, as you see here. Asparagus is one of the finest tastes in vegetables, and one of the most expensive – two good incentives to grow your own. It is easy to grow, but needs plenty of space; in fact rather more than is often recommended, especially as plants grow older.

In this video, I show you the full process of growing asparagus from seed in module trays, potting on young plants, and planting them out into the garden, alongside an honest comparison with planting bought crowns.

The difference between sowing seed and planting crowns is mostly in time to cropping. A year’s wait can be saved, but sowing your own is satisfying and should make for healthy plants. I share real examples of variable germination, how to deal with poor seed, and which varieties have performed best in my own garden.

From sowing in late winter, seedlings are potted on through spring and planted out in early summer.
Summer growth should, after about three years, resemble a mini-forest about 2m (6') high, dark enough underneath to prevent germination of too many weeds. Any that do appear must be removed before they seed, so that the soil becomes and stays clean, making the bed easier to maintain.

After about three years, plants should be cropping well. I also show what underperforming asparagus looks like, how soil type affects growth, and why some bought crowns fail to thrive even after several seasons. You’ll see why spacing, drainage, and long-term soil health matter so much.

In late autumn or winter, dying top growth can be cut off and composted, or left to rot on the surface if there are few weeds to hoe in springtime. The most difficult weed can be asparagus itself, from its own fertile berries that drop off or are excreted elsewhere by birds. All-male hybrids, such as Jersey Knight F1, mostly have no berries and tend to yield heavier spears.

We also look at seasonal care, harvesting timelines, autumn maintenance, and how asparagus fits into a whole-year garden cycle, including interplanting with crops such as spinach.
Have fun growing asparagus — it rewards patience with decades of harvests.

title: How to Grow Turnips Early & Late | Sowing, Transplanting & Harvest Tips | No Dig
text:
Turnips can be wonderfully sweet and crisp when you grow the right varieties — especially the Japanese Hakurei types. In this video I show how to sow, transplant, protect, and harvest turnips for both early spring and late autumn success, using simple No Dig principles.

Through 2025, Carly and I filmed many sequences to illustrate how to grow different vegetables. I hope that you enjoy this look at turnips.

These fast-growing brassicas produce beautiful tender greens as well as crisp bulbs that can be eaten raw like a radish. Multi-sowing gives great harvests from small space, and you can pick the biggest roots first while the rest continue to grow.

Sow mid-February to 10th March under cover, or mid-March outside with fleece, for harvesting in May. Sow again in August for welcome autumn harvests. Avoid sowing late spring to midsummer when they are more likely to flower early.

Fleece helps protect against cold nights and cabbage root fly, while No Dig soil encourages quick establishment, good moisture retention, and healthy roots with minimal pest damage.

title: How To Grow Globe Artichokes from Seed | No Dig Method
text:
Grow globe artichokes from seed, sow them mid February and not yet!. See how their growth can be surprisingly rapid, for harvests in the first summer, depending on your climate.

In this video I show why this method works so well using the no dig approach. When sown in mid-February and planted early, artichokes can crop in their first summer, even though they are perennial plants.

These are Gros Vert de Laon, an old French variety, and I demonstrate sowing in modules, potting on, spacing, planting into a no dig bed, and finally harvesting. Growing from seed gives healthy, vigorous plants, with some natural variation in size and timing.

You’ll see how seed-grown artichokes establish quickly, tolerate dry conditions, and perform well when planted early. I also share practical tips on compost use, spacing, frost tolerance, and harvesting, based on my own experience.

These are Gros Vert de Laon, and they are perennial. 
Find them in my February seed pack by Premier Seeds Direct: https://premierseedsdirect.com/product/charles-dowding-january-february-collection/ 

Growing globe artichokes from seed removes worries about overwintering plants and suits many climates, with small adjustments depending on where you garden.

title: Festive Brussels Sprouts & Their Delicious Tops | No Dig Winter Harvest
text:
Oddly, in the UK at least, Brussels sprouts are considered a key vegetable for this time of year, yet I struggle to meet many people who like them!

In fact, they are delicious when grown in healthy soil without fertilisers or other synthetic chemicals. The no dig biology really helps. And then you get a treat like the plant top I show you.

At this festive time of year, the Brussels sprout top is one of the most delicious parts of the plant. Cutting it does stop further sprout growth, but by late December that matters little, and it’s often worth harvesting before pigeons arrive as the weather turns colder.

The secrets for growing best Brussels are:
1 Soil with decent amounts of organic matter on top,
2 Choose F1 hybrids, not open pollinated which have not been maintained, this is Brigitte F1,
3 No dig for healthy soil biology – like, this bed already grew a fantastic crop of carrots in the spring, before these sprout plants which went in 17th June.

These are Brigitte F1 Brussels sprouts, ready now for festive meals, and I hope you enjoy both the sprouts and their tops as much as I do.

Filmed by Nicola Smith, 23rd December, zone 8 climate.

title: Mulching No Dig Beds for Winter & Spring | Compost, Woodchip & Soil Care
text:
When compost is applied on soil, as food and cover, two or three months before sowing or planting, it will be broken up into a crumbly state by weather. And some will have disappeared into the soil already when our seeds or plants are ready to go in the ground. 

In addition, compost contains nutrients that are not soluble in water. They remain near the surface until used by the biological networks common in no dig soil. You never need to use fertilisers because the nutrients are there and available as needed, thanks to mycorrhizal and rhizophagy networks.

Plus, any lumpy, wet and sticky compost breaks open nicely on top of soil, better than it can when in a heap.

A no digger can step out in the first mild weather after frost has lifted, to rejoice in a wonderful soft surface that is ready for seeds and transplants. This is particularly the case when a bed layout allows access from say 40cm / 16 in pathways, leaving the much larger growing area soft and friable (see Chapter 2), and with no wooden sides in the way.
. 
No moisture is lost to cultivation, and you have a tilth for sowing into whatever the weather, even if wet. In the medium and longer term, there is the huge bonus of fewer weeds.

#nodig  #mulching  #soilhealth  #compost  #nodiggarden  #gardeningtips

title: No Dig Explained, for Healthier Soil, Fewer Weeds, Bigger Harvests
text:
Don’t be afraid of failing. Trust the soil, grow what you want to eat, and enjoy the journey.

What actually happens in soil if you leave it alone? I show a hole I dug in a no dig bed and explain how the result is healthier soil and plants, fewer weeds, and better harvests — when you maintain a surface mulch of organic matter.

This video explores why no dig works, how to begin, and what happens in soil when you stop disturbing it. It’s a very simple process, and I show how you can start out, control weeds, feed your plants, and keep the garden productive all year.

My garden here in Somerset is now in its 13th year of no dig – judge for yourself.

No dig brings liberation on two levels. Physically it’s considerably easier, and mentally — even emotionally — it brings you closer to nature and helps you understand how we can help soil to improve, even while it’s growing fantastic food for us.

Traditional gardening advice is based on a hidden assumption: that soil is being disturbed every year. That’s a damaging thing, and once we stop doing it, practices such as rotation, which have been used to minimise disease and growth problems, are no longer necessary.

You’ll see how simple it can be:
- How to start no dig and control weeds from the first year.
- Why firm, stable soil structure means you can even walk on your beds.
- Why no dig is simple, straightforward, quick, and easy.
- And why small is beautiful — even one bed can get you started.

However ... it does not always go to plan if autumn temperatures are unusually warm, as this year, meaning the spring cabbage look too advanced to me. And the spring onions have been massively attacked by Allium leaf miner, despite the mesh cover and the miners are proving a really difficult (new) pest here.

title: Winter Vegetables in the Polytunnel 🌱
text:
Polytunnel vegetables through winter (unheated) are way easier with no dig:
1 speed of planting, 
2 steady nutrient supply,
3 reduced weed growth. 
This is YEAR 13 of the same salad plants and garlic in this soil, and we are picking at least half of them weekly when it’s mild as now.
Mostly the harvest is for selling in my salad mix, a few larger ones are for cooking and juicing.

Its zone 8 here in winter, and night temperatures in December 2025 are currently 4°C, and 12°C by day - and not much higher in the tunnel because sunshine is lacking. At least there’s no lashing of rain or blowing of wind on these plants. I keep the doors open to maintain steady ventilation and reduce mildew. #nodig #wintervegetables #polytunnelgrowing

title: December No-Dig Garden Tour: Winter Harvest & Frost-Hardy Crops
text:
Welcome to a beautiful December tour. It’s a lovely day and there are still some gorgeous vegetables to show — Savoy cabbage, white sprouting, kale, winter salads and lamb’s lettuce. These beds are the result of doing things at different times in the back end of the year, so there’s still plenty of interest now.

We’ve been down to –5.6°C here, and that frost has killed the self-seeded marigolds, while some endive has suffered after the unusually warm November weather we had, with daytime temperatures even up to 17°C.

There’s a nice little winter store of carrots, and these carrots are feeding the deer now — they’re only eating the greens, not the carrot itself, and that doesn’t matter at this point. In the shed, there’s some lovely celeriac, beetroot and chicory keeping well for winter food.
Some broad beans are small and just right to overwinter, while others were sown earlier — like a cover crop — to rot into the ground as they’re killed. Brussels sprouts are looking steady, garlic has had a bit of wood ash against rust, and purple sprouting broccoli is smaller this year after flea beetle, but still looking good.

It’s late November and we’re really coming into the quiet time now. There’s no worries about sowing or planting. You can enjoy the garden — it’s a lovely time of year.

🎥 Filmed 28th November 2025 by Nicola Smith

title: Growing Vegetables in Barrel Planters: From August Planting to October Abundance
text:
Ian and Jessica from Gardenscapes Uprooted in Yorkshire contacted me recently about trying one of their barrel planters here. It’s made from old whiskey barrels, and this planter is two barrels just screwed together.

These are two stacked barrels in the Small Garden, filled and planted on 7th August, so nine weeks before we filmed again on 10th October.

I wanted to show the principle of vertical gardening, making more of available space, and to look at both the potential benefits and some drawbacks. We set it up on soil because you need holes in the bottom, both for drainage and for colonisation of the contents by microbes already in the soil.

Each barrel contains around 440 litres of material. I used a mix of ⅓ topsoil and ⅔ compost — in my case, half homemade compost and half mushroom compost — with about 1 kg of fine rockdust and half a kilogram of dried seaweed for trace elements. Watering well during and after filling is important. Much of the material was dry, so I danced on the compost and soil to consolidate it before planting, which happened on the same day.

The barrels have five-centimetre holes — just enough to get your finger in and pop in a plug plant. We added spinach, lettuce, chicory, kohlrabi, sage, rosemary, Florence fennel and a few strawberries.

Watering was mainly done from the top, letting the water make a kind of stream so some of it ran into the holes. Growth was good even without much watering, because the wood holds moisture well.

When filming again on 10th October, about eight weeks later, it had been a lovely autumn of growth. Plants growing at a funny angle were still holding well, the fennel are starting to do their thing, and although most of the brassicas had got quite eaten by caterpillars, there was still some kohlrabi left. The herbs, especially sage and rosemary, looked strong.

I was pleased to see it hasn’t sunk too much, which shows the value of including soil — it would have sunk more if it were all compost. As plants finish, you can twist them out and replant. The height means it’s something to consider in winter winds.

Gardenscapes Uprooted make these in Yorkshire:
https://www.gardenscapesuprooted.com

You can also buy your own barrels and drill two-inch, five-centimetre holes in the same pattern. So many possibilities.

🎥 Filmed 10th October 2025 by Carly Dutton-Edwards

title: Polytunnel & Greenhouse Changeover | No-Dig Winter Planting, Watering & Ventilation – October 2025
text:
Mid-October brings a changeover from summer to winter crops in both the polytunnel and greenhouse.

This film shows Part 1 on 10 October and Part 2 on 24 October 2025 – a full transition from the last harvests of summer plants to fresh winter salads.

By 12th October, all summer plants are removed for compost. The beds are re-measured and levelled with no new compost added, then given a big watering before planting.

In the second part, you see planting which happened 15th October, 9 days earlier.. They were  from sowings made on 11th September for lettuce and endive, and 16th September for brassicas, all frost-hardy plants for winter harvests.

Ventilation – doors stay open through winter except in gales.
Watering – about once a week in November, twice in December, and sometimes none in January, then increasing again in spring.
*Occasional fleece goes over beds before hard frosts*
Weeding is minimal, though there are many self-seeded French marigolds, which frost will kill naturally.

The first pick will be in late November, then every one or two weeks until late April.

This video also shows: 
1how to re-wet dry soil, 
2 how the no-dig system supports repeat cropping in the same beds, over many years, 
3 how to plan a two-crop year undercover (October–May and May–October).

title: Make Your First No Dig Bed | Start Growing Your Own Food | No Dig Day 2025
text:
Here at Homeacres, I show you how simple it is to make your first no dig bed — starting from a patch of weeds and transforming it into a thriving space for growing food. Using just cardboard and compost, I’ll share tips on dealing with perennial weeds, keeping neat edges, and creating healthy soil for abundant harvests all year round.

No Dig Day is our celebration of this wonderful method of growing healthy abundant vegetables.

title: Storing Celeriac for Winter | Harvest or Leave in the Ground? | No Dig Gardening Tips
text:
It’s been a dry summer, yet these celeriac have grown really well here at Homeacres No Dig Garden in Somerset, UK.

Filmed on 24th October, I look at whether it’s better to leave them in the ground through winter or harvest and store them in a shed.

Celeriac will take frost down to about –10 °C, though left outside they may lose quality from disease or animal damage.

So I usually lift them before mid-December and keep them in boxes in the shed. Being such a dense root, they keep well for three to four months without shrivelling.

🎥 Filmed by Carly Dutton-Edwards at Homeacres No Dig Garden, Somerset UK

More on no dig growing and winter harvests at charlesdowding.co.uk

title: From $600 Power Bills to Self-Sufficient POWER - My MEGA Solar & Battery System!
text:
I was paying over $600 a month for electricity… and the power still kept going out. Storms, grid outages, and planned shutdowns were becoming a regular problem. So I decided to go all in on a mega solar and battery system to see if I could become self-sufficient in electricity.

👉 Free Solar & Battery Assessment:
https://resinc.com.au/selfsufficientme/
(This includes access to current rebates and exclusive offers available through my link.)
In this video I show you:

☀️ Why I decided to install a massive solar system
🔋 The installation of 62 solar panels + a 50kWh battery system
⚡ How our home handles blackouts now
💰 Our first quarterly power bill after the install
🏡 Whether we are actually self-sufficient in power

After four months running the system, our electricity usage from the grid has dropped by 81.7%, and our power bill for the quarter fell from over $2000 down to $541.

This system includes:

• 62 Longi 475W solar panels
• 25kW Sungrow inverter
• 50kWh Sungrow battery storage (two stacks)
• 3-phase home power setup
• Additional 5kW inverter in the shed

While we may not be 100% off-grid yet, we’re extremely close — and the blackout protection alone has already been worth it.

title: Can the Hansa C21 Chip Our Whole Orchard? (4 Days of Real-World Testing)
text:
Can the Hansa C21 chipper really handle an entire orchard after a major hard prune?

In this video, I put Hansa’s biggest chipper in the acreage range to a proper real-world test. Over four full days, we chipped a huge pile of orchard prunings — including some branches and logs that were honestly bigger than the recommended size.

The result? The C21 didn’t miss a beat. It powered through everything we fed it, started easily, ran consistently, and handled the workload far better than I expected.

After using it hard for several days straight, I’d be comfortable recommending the C21 to anyone managing acreage, orchards, or large properties.

If you’ve ever wondered whether a serious chipper like this is worth it for real garden and orchard work — this video should help you decide.

I Quit My Job at 38 to Be a Stay-at-Home Dad… Best Decision Ever!
text:
I quit my job at 38 to become a stay-at-home dad… and it turned out to be the best decision of my life! In 2008, I left a secure career to care for my kids full-time. It wasn’t easy, but it completely changed the direction of my life. In this video, I share the 5 biggest reasons why leaving my job to be a home dad was the best move I ever made. title: The History of Tomatoes & Why Eating Them was Considered Dangerous
text:
In this video, I dig into the wild and surprising history of tomatoes — from being feared as poisonous in Europe to becoming a beloved staple on pizzas and pastas around the world. title: Landscaping only for humans? There is a better way.
text:
So much of our landscape is human-centered, and even human-exclusive. What would happen if we all landscaped in a way that benefited more than just the human animal that lives on your land?

Edit: I misspoke slightly when I said plant things only for nature such as Oaks. That's just how I personally use oaks. Obviously you can eat acorns (flour), etc. I just don't do that (no time), right now at least - I may do it in the future though. For now, planting something like Oaks is just me being a servant to the natural biome.

title: What's the big side-project I'm working on? May update

title: Fertility plan for a food forest
text:
I had a recent speaking engagement at the Simcoe Master Gardeners association. A question came up: what do I do for fertility in a food forest?

It led to a very healthy and long discussion, and at the end of the night I had many people (master gardeners no less) tell me that this portion of the discussion really opened their minds. I thought that it would be an interesting video, so here you go, a 20 minute discussion on where the fertility in my food forest comes from. Why does it look so green and lush? Why? Because in permaculture, we do the OPPOSITE of what many people in conventional gardening do.

______________________

title: The 2 Golden Rules of Gardening - this will change how you garden forever (update)
text:
An updated visit to my 2 golden rules of gardening:

1) Maximize Photosynthesis
2) Never bare soil

After doing this for a while, I would add a 3rd, which is "embrace nature". That sometimes means embracing chaos. But with chaos comes resilience (oddly enough).

Other videos mentioned in this one:

Soil microbiology guide:
https://youtu.be/LO-ostC1q-4

Guide to permaculture guilds:
https://youtu.be/XLPUN2wGbwA

The original 2 golden rules video:
https://youtu.be/cFLyGVhu0bY

Or for a much funnier man explaining what's wrong with current farming practices, check out this recent video from John Oliver (yes, the comedian) on Corn. This one is a must watch (LOL): 
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MI78WOW_u-Q

title: This 7 year old permaculture food forest will blow you away!
text:
It's been 7 years this month on my property, although to be honest the first two years were fairly slow... only 4 trees. I had an explosion of effort in the next 4 years, and have expanded the food forest in ways that have changed my life in profound ways.

Now is the time to work on your self sufficiency and build something like this, even if just small pieces of it, somewhere on your land. This is life changing, and I hope many other people start their permaculture journeys in these crucial years we find ourselves in. 

Today's video walks around the property, discussing some ways we grow with nature in mind, how we share our bounty with wild animals, how we focus our efforts on growing the insect biome, and how fertility follows when we follow these principles. I hope you enjoy, and consider supporting us as below:

title: Where I should put new raised beds?
text:
Where would you build it? Feel free to leave a comment, and also consider participating in the poll (see recent community post), to see what others think also. https://www.youtube.com/@CanadianPermacultureLegacy/community


I thought it would be fun to share some thoughts that I go through when deciding where to put new beds in my garden. I will take a walkaround tour of the backyard showing potential spots, and showing plants and guilds as we go by them.

Then you try to think... where would you put these beds if it were you? Also, are there any things I didn't discuss that you consider when making these decisions? Let us know!

Some things I didn't discuss, but will add here:

Water. I didn't discuss this because access to water is fairly easy at all 5 sites. Site 1 has close proximity to the artesian well overflow where I have a solar sump pump located. Site 2, same thing. Site 3, is mid way between the artesian well overflow and the pond, for easy access to both sources. Site 4 is right next to the pond, for really easy high-nutrient water. Site 5 is relatively close to the pond, but there is also an outdoor faucet there (which feeds from a well, not city water).

______________________

title: Our changing climate, politics, overshoot, collapse, and how you should focus your effort positively
text:
Today I'm going to hit some pretty big topics. This one is going to make you scared, anxious, excited, engaged, angry, and inspired all at the same time. We need action more than ever, and although this video is going to be contentious to some, I hope people can approach this video, and the times we live in, with open minds. And whenever in doubt, lets default to the scientists and science, and not to social media influencers (myself included).  Lets have a return to sanity, in these, which are the most important years in the human history.

title: Science: Discussing EVs, E-bikes, E-scooters, and some latest climate science
text:

title: This is a major change to how I would manage a new food forest
text:
When I started in permaculture, reading all the books, listening to podcasts, watching movies and youtube, I came up with strategies on how to start my food forest. Now that I'm ~7 years into this, these are some ways that I would have changed the way I started my food forest. These tips can be great if you are just getting started.

Keep in mind, we're probably about 10 years away from major disruptions to live. Climate change, economy, end of oil, energy scarcity, refugee crisis, food scarcity, and more. Starting a food forest now, you still have time to secure major resilience in your life, and your family's future.

title: Food Forest tour, and a small discussion on hydroponics and permaculture - Late fall, 2023.
text:
It's been a while, due to illness, but we're back at it. 

This spring, I mentioned that I would try to take the same pathway through the food forest, pond, wildflower hill and old man walking trail, so that you could see the entire property, and see the same sections at various times of the year. Today we continue that tour-experiment. Let me know if you like that idea, and like these videos.

I also end the video on a discussion of small scale hydroponics (and other such things) and if they belong in permaculture. If they are "organic", or "sustainable". This is just a line of thinking that I had, because I've seen this topic brought up in various other locations before. I.e. "Hydroponics can't be permaculture because there's a pump that draws electricity", etc. Or "hydroponics can't be organic". It doesn't help that both the terms "permaculture" and "organic" have various meanings to various people. But I give my thoughts at the end of the video. Let me know your thoughts in the comments!

I hope you enjoyed this tour, and I hope you've had a wonderful and rewarding growing season! Much love to my permie family!

Keith
______________________
